learning_objectives: Apply the technology adoption lifecycle to evaluate emerging communications technologies
Distinguish between hype and genuine potential in new technologies
Develop a framework for evaluating when to adopt new tools and platforms
readings: Gartner Hype Cycle for Digital Marketing and Advertising (current year)

lecture_notes.introduction: New technologies constantly promise to revolutionize communications. Most fail. Some transform everything. This course teaches you to evaluate emerging technologies critically, identify genuine opportunities before they become mainstream, and develop adaptive strategies that incorporate innovation without chasing every trend.
key_concepts: Technology adoption lifecycle: innovators, early adopters, early majority, late majority, laggards
Gartner Hype Cycle: innovation trigger, peak of inflated expectations, trough of disillusionment, slope of enlightenment, plateau of productivity
Evaluation framework: problem it solves, audience readiness, implementation cost, competitive advantage window
First-mover vs. fast-follower: when early adoption is advantageous vs. when waiting is smarter
Technology scouting: systematic monitoring of emerging technologies relevant to communications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating three current emerging technologies using the Gartner Hype Cycle and adoption framework: determining where each sits, what the realistic timeline to mainstream is, and whether early investment is justified

review.value: The phase of the Hype Cycle where early experiments fail to deliver on inflated promises, media coverage becomes negative, and investment drops; technologies that survive this phase emerge with realistic expectations and proven use cases

The period during which early adopters of a new technology gain competitive advantage through audience novelty, platform algorithm favorability, and lower costs; the window closes as adoption becomes mainstream and competition intensifies

learning_objectives: Apply generative AI tools effectively for research, ideation, drafting, and optimization
Identify the limitations and risks of AI-generated content in professional contexts
Develop AI-augmented workflows that enhance rather than replace human creativity
readings: McKinsey, 'The State of AI in Marketing and Sales' (annual report)

lecture_notes.introduction: Generative AI is the most transformative technology to hit communications since social media. Understanding how to use it effectively, ethically, and strategically is an immediate professional requirement, not a future consideration.
key_concepts: AI use cases in communications: research assistance, content drafting, A/B test generation, personalization at scale, data analysis
Prompt engineering: crafting inputs that produce useful outputs, including context, constraints, and quality criteria
AI limitations: hallucination, bias, lack of genuine understanding, inability to verify claims
Human-AI collaboration: using AI to accelerate rather than replace the creative and strategic process
Quality control: fact-checking, brand voice alignment, and editorial review of AI-assisted content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an AI-augmented content workflow: using AI for research and outline generation, human creativity for angle and storytelling, AI for draft generation, and human editing for quality, voice, and fact-checking
setup_requirements: Access to a generative AI tool

review.value: When an AI model generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information, including fabricated statistics, non-existent sources, and false claims; all AI-generated content must be fact-checked before publication

The practice of crafting AI inputs (prompts) that include sufficient context, clear instructions, quality criteria, and constraints to produce useful outputs; the quality of the output is directly proportional to the quality of the prompt

class_schedule.topic: Immersive Media: AR, VR, and Spatial Computing
learning_objectives: Evaluate augmented and virtual reality as communications and marketing channels
Design immersive brand experiences using AR tools accessible to non-developers
Assess the current viability and future trajectory of spatial computing for communications
readings: Meta, 'Introduction to Spatial Computing for Business'

lecture_notes.introduction: Immersive media moves communications from flat screens to three-dimensional experiences. While full VR remains niche, AR is already mainstream through smartphone cameras and social media filters. Understanding how to create immersive experiences gives communicators a powerful tool for engagement.
key_concepts: AR vs. VR vs. MR: augmented (digital overlay on real world), virtual (fully digital environment), mixed (interactive digital-physical blend)
AR in marketing: product try-on (IKEA, Warby Parker), social filters (Instagram, Snapchat), location-based experiences
VR for communications: virtual events, training simulations, immersive storytelling, virtual showrooms
Spatial computing: Apple Vision Pro and the potential shift from 2D to 3D digital interaction
Accessibility and adoption barriers: hardware cost, content creation complexity, motion sickness, privacy concerns
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating an AR Instagram filter using Meta Spark Studio (free tool) for a brand promotion, from concept through design to testing on mobile devices
setup_requirements: Brand brief for AR experience design

review.value: Augmented reality experiences accessed through a web browser without requiring an app download, dramatically reducing friction for marketing activations; users simply scan a QR code or click a link to enter the AR experience
Using VR or 360-degree video to place the audience inside a story rather than watching it from outside, creating emotional impact through presence and spatial awareness that flat video cannot achieve

learning_objectives: Design voice-first content for smart speakers and voice assistants
Create conversational AI experiences using chatbot and voice bot platforms
Evaluate voice search optimization as an extension of traditional SEO
readings: Pearl, Designing Voice User Interfaces, Ch. 1-4
Google, 'Actions on Google' developer documentation (overview sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Voice interfaces represent a fundamental shift from visual to auditory interaction. As smart speakers, voice assistants, and conversational AI become more capable, communications professionals must learn to create content and experiences for screenless interactions.
key_concepts: Voice search behavior: longer, conversational queries vs. typed keyword searches
Voice content optimization: featured snippets, FAQ schema, conversational answer formatting
Chatbot design: conversational flows, personality design, fallback handling, handoff to humans
Voice branding: audio logos, voice character, sonic identity for voice-first touchpoints
Limitations: voice recognition accuracy, context understanding, privacy concerns with always-on devices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a customer service chatbot flow for a retail brand: mapping conversation trees, writing natural dialogue, handling edge cases, and implementing a human handoff protocol
setup_requirements: Chatbot building platform (Dialogflow, ManyChat, or similar)

review.value: Adapting SEO strategy for voice queries: targeting conversational long-tail keywords, optimizing for featured snippets (which voice assistants read aloud), and structuring content to answer questions directly in natural language

The systematic development of a brand's audio identity including audio logo, hold music, notification sounds, voice assistant persona, and voice characteristics; as voice interfaces grow, sonic identity becomes as important as visual identity

learning_objectives: Develop a technology adoption roadmap for a communications organization
Build a technology evaluation and experimentation framework for ongoing innovation
Present emerging technology recommendations with realistic ROI projections

lecture_notes.introduction: The goal is not to adopt every new technology but to build an organizational capacity for evaluating and adopting the right technologies at the right time. This final session synthesizes the course into a framework for perpetual technology readiness.
key_concepts: Technology roadmap: phasing adoption into immediate (proven ROI), near-term (pilot-ready), and exploratory (monitor)
Innovation budget: allocating 10-20% of resources to experimentation with emerging technologies
Build vs. buy vs. partner: determining the right approach for technology adoption based on organizational capability
Change management: preparing teams for new technology adoption including training and transition planning
Failure tolerance: accepting that some technology experiments will fail and building that into the innovation process
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a 12-month emerging technology roadmap for a communications agency: evaluating five technologies, categorizing by readiness, proposing pilot programs for two, and calculating projected ROI

review.value: A dedicated allocation (10-20% of resources) for experimenting with emerging technologies, separate from the operational budget; this protects innovation from being cut during efficiency reviews while establishing accountability for experimentation outcomes

A classification system rating technologies from level 1 (basic research) through level 9 (proven in production); communications leaders use this to categorize technologies as exploratory, pilot-ready, or deployment-ready for strategic planning

questions.question: Describe the Gartner Hype Cycle phases and identify which phase generative AI for content creation is currently in. Justify your assessment.
questions.answer: The five phases are: (1) Innovation Trigger - initial breakthrough generates media attention. (2) Peak of Inflated Expectations - early publicity produces unrealistic expectations. (3) Trough of Disillusionment - failures and limitations become apparent. (4) Slope of Enlightenment - realistic use cases emerge with proven results. (5) Plateau of Productivity - mainstream adoption based on proven value. Generative AI for content creation is transitioning from Peak of Inflated Expectations to the Slope of Enlightenment. Initial excitement led to claims that AI would replace writers and designers, but practical experience has revealed limitations (hallucination, generic output, brand voice issues). Organizations are now developing realistic workflows that use AI as an augmentation tool with human oversight, representing the early slope of enlightenment.

rubric.key: Identifies current phase for generative AI

questions.question: What is AI hallucination, and why is it a particular risk for communications professionals compared to other AI users?
questions.answer: AI hallucination occurs when a generative AI model produces plausible-sounding but factually incorrect content, including fabricated statistics, non-existent sources, fake quotes, and false claims. It is a particular risk for communications professionals because: (1) Communications content is published to audiences who may trust and act on it. (2) False claims in marketing or PR can create legal liability and regulatory violations. (3) Brand credibility depends on accuracy; a single fabricated statistic can destroy trust. (4) Communications content is designed to persuade, meaning false information presented persuasively through professional channels causes disproportionate harm compared to internal-only use.

rubric.key: Explains why the risk is heightened for this profession

questions.question: Explain the difference between AR and VR and describe one practical marketing application for each that is viable today with current technology.
questions.answer: AR (Augmented Reality) overlays digital elements onto the real world viewed through a device (smartphone, smart glasses). VR (Virtual Reality) creates a fully digital environment that replaces the real world, accessed through a headset. Practical AR application: virtual product try-on, such as IKEA Place allowing customers to see how furniture looks in their home through their smartphone camera, driving purchase confidence and reducing returns. Practical VR application: virtual property tours for real estate, allowing prospective buyers to walk through homes remotely using a VR headset, expanding the potential buyer pool beyond local geography.

questions.question: What is voice search optimization, and describe two ways it differs from traditional text-based SEO.
questions.answer: Voice search optimization is adapting content and SEO strategy for spoken queries directed at voice assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant, Siri). Two key differences from text SEO: (1) Query structure: voice searches are longer and conversational ('What's the best Italian restaurant near me that's open right now?') vs. typed searches ('Italian restaurant near me'). This requires targeting natural language long-tail keywords and question-based phrases. (2) Single answer format: voice assistants typically read one answer (the featured snippet) rather than presenting a list of results. Winning position zero is critical for voice because there is no position two in a spoken response.

major_assignments.title: Emerging Technology Communications Strategy
major_assignments.description: Select an emerging technology and develop a comprehensive strategy for a brand to adopt it as a communications channel. Include technology evaluation, audience readiness assessment, pilot program design, content strategy, and ROI projection.
objectives: Evaluate an emerging technology using rigorous assessment frameworks
Design a pilot program that tests the technology with manageable risk
Create a content strategy tailored to the technology's unique capabilities
Project ROI with realistic assumptions and measurement methodology
deliverables: Technology evaluation (3-4 pages) using Hype Cycle, adoption lifecycle, and SWOT analysis
Audience readiness assessment based on target demographic technology adoption patterns
Pilot program design (4-5 pages) with objectives, timeline, budget, and success criteria
Content strategy for the new technology channel with 3-5 sample content concepts
ROI projection with assumptions stated and measurement framework defined

criteria: Assessment uses established frameworks rigorously
Analysis distinguishes hype from genuine capability
Competitive landscape is assessed
Risks and limitations are honestly evaluated
Pilot objectives are SMART and achievable
Timeline and budget are realistic
Success criteria are measurable and meaningful
Failure scenario planning is included
Strategy leverages the technology's unique capabilities
Content concepts are creative and feasible
Audience experience is well-designed
Brand consistency is maintained in the new format
Assumptions are stated and reasonable
Measurement methodology is sound
Projection includes both optimistic and conservative scenarios
Break-even timeline is calculated
Rigorous technology evaluation demonstrating critical thinking
Well-designed pilot program with clear success criteria
Creative content strategy leveraging technology capabilities
Realistic ROI projection with transparent methodology

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on emerging technologies and evaluation frameworks

weekly_technology_labs.description: Weekly hands-on experiments with emerging communication technologies

major_assignment.description: Emerging technology communications strategy and pilot design

midterm_examination.description: Technology evaluation examination with live assessment exercise

final_presentation.description: Final technology strategy presentation with professional critique

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for technology lab sessions. Demonstrations require in-person participation. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Technology pilot proposals cannot be accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Technology lab experiments may involve team work. All graded deliverables are individual. Sharing strategy documents is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Technology assessments must cite sources. ROI projections must state assumptions. Fabricating data or market research is academic fraud.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop, smartphone, and willingness to create accounts on emerging platforms. Specific tools vary by week and will be announced in advance. All required tools have free tiers.
